questions.question: Which of the following is a correct three-figure bearing for a point exactly North-West of a starting position?
questions.explanation:
Bearings are measured clockwise from North ($000°$). North-West is three-quarters of the way around ($270°$ for West + $45°$), totaling $315°$.

**Common mistake:** A student might measure anticlockwise from North or confuse North-West with South-West ($225°$).

questions.question: The bearing of Town $Y$ from Town $X$ is $072°$. Calculate the bearing of Town $X$ from Town $Y$.
questions.explanation:
To find the back bearing when the initial bearing is less than $180°$, add $180°$. $72 + 180 = 252°$.

**Common mistake:** The student might subtract the angle from $180°$ (finding the supplementary angle) instead of adding it to the North line.

questions.question: An observer looks at the top of a flagpole. Which term describes the angle between their horizontal line of sight and their line of sight to the top of the pole?
questions.explanation:
The angle measured upward from the horizontal to an object is defined as the angle of elevation.

**Common mistake:** Students may confuse elevation (upward) with depression (downward).

questions.question: A map has a scale of $1 : 25000$. What actual distance in kilometres is represented by $4\text{ cm}$ on the map?
questions.explanation:
$4\text{ cm} \times 25000 = 100000\text{ cm}$. Converting to km: $100000 ÷ 100 ÷ 1000 = 1\text{ km}$.

**Common mistake:** A student might miscalculate the number of zeros when converting from centimetres to kilometres.

questions.question: John is at the top of a cliff looking down at a boat. The angle between the horizontal and his line of sight to the boat is $25°$. What is this angle called?
questions.explanation:
Angles measured downward from the horizontal to an object are angles of depression.

**Common mistake:** A student might assume any angle involving height is 'elevation' regardless of the direction of sight.
